{{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|qid=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Hoori|qid=Q1051960}}|father={{ill|Oshihomimi|qid=Q462555}}|mother={{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|qid=Q11110848}}|relatives={{ill|honoakari|qid=Q10940411}}|organization={{ill|Chijin Godai|qid=Q18337308}}; {{ill|Three generations of Hyuga|qid=Q56347075}}|resting_place={{ill|E no Misasagi|qid=Q11411834}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1056017]] Wikidata name: Ninigi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto god}} {{For|the asteroid|10619 Ninigi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Ninigi-no-Mikoto | consorts = [[Konohanasakuya-hime]] (wife) | children = Hoderi, Hosuseri, [[Hoori]], Hikohohodemi, Tamanoya | parents =[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (father) [[Takuhadachiji-hime]] (mother); {{ill|Oshihomimi|qid=Q462555}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|qid=Q11110848}}| image =Ninigi.png; File:Ninigi.png| gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | consort = {{ill|Konohananosakuya-bime|qid=Q1781862}} | offspring = {{ill|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491}}; {{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|qid=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Hoori|qid=Q1051960}} | member_of = {{ill|Chijin Godai|qid=Q18337308}}; {{ill|Three generations of Hyuga|qid=Q56347075}} | equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Hoori|qid=Q1051960}}; {{ill|Nagarjuna|qid=Q171195}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Ninigi-no-Mikoto'''|瓊瓊杵尊|lead=yes}} is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="Mythopedia2">{{Cite web|title=Ninigi|url=https://mythopedia.com/japanese-mythology/gods/ninigi/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Mythopedia|language=en}}</ref> (-no-Mikoto here is an honorific title applied to the names of Japanese gods; Ninigi is the specific god's name.) Grandson of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Borgen |first1=Robert |last2=Ury |first2=Marian |title=Readable Japanese Mythology: Selections from Nihon shoki and Kojiki |journal=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |date=April 1990 |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=61–97 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/489230 |access-date=12 April 2020 |publisher=American Association of Teachers of Japanese |doi=10.2307/489230 |jstor=489230 |format=PDF}}</ref> Ninigi is regarded according to Japanese mythology as the great-grandfather of Japan’s first emperor, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref name="WillisR">{{cite book |editor1-last=Willis |editor1-first=Roy |title=World Mythology: The Illustrated Guide |date=2006 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0-19530752-8 |pages=114, 116, 120}}</ref><ref name="Mythopedia2" /><ref name="Huffman">{{cite book |last1=Huffman |first1=James L. |title=Japan in World History |date=2010 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=13}}</ref> The [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|three sacred treasures]] brought with Ninigi from Heaven and divine ancestry established the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese Imperial Family]].<ref name="Sykes">{{cite book |last1=Sykes |first1=Egerton |editor1-last=Kendall |editor1-first=Alan |title=Who's Who Non-Classical Mythology |date=1993 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=141}}</ref><ref name="Ashkenazi">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |page=137 |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/136/mode/2up?q=Ninigi |access-date=27 October 2020}}</ref>
The three generations of ''kami'' starting with Ninigi are sometimes referred to as the [[:en:Three generations of Hyūga|Three generations of Hyūga]], they are said to represent a transitional period between the heavenly ''kami'' and the first emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/|access-date=2021-09-13|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Name and etymology == [[File:Ninigi-no-mikoto_statues_at_Kunimigaoka.jpg|thumb|220px|Ninigi-no-mikoto statue at Kunimigaoka Viewpoint]] Ninigi-no-Mikoto (瓊瓊杵尊), means "The Great God Ninigi." Another name of his is '''Ame-nigishi-kuni-nigishi-amatsuhiko-hiko-ho-no-ninigi-no-Mikoto''' (天邇岐志国邇岐志天津日高日子番能邇邇芸命) or "The Great God Ninigi, of the Imperial State, The Child of the Sun of Many Talents." Ninigi is speculated to be translated as "beloved jeweled mallet."<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
== Myths ==
=== Birth === Ninigi was born from [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[Takuhadachiji-hime]]. [[Takamimusubi]] treated him with special affection and nurtured him with great regard.<ref>{{Cite book|chapter-url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_II|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|chapter=Book II }}</ref>
==== Sent to rule ==== Depending on the version Amaterasu sends Ninigi to rule either after his father refuses the offer, after several failures, or to replace [[Ōkuninushi]] after his troubled rule.<ref name="Ashkenazi 2003">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |isbn=9781576074671 |page=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/222 222] |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk |url-access=registration |chapter=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
In many stories, Ninigi receives three gifts. The sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]], the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]], and the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Ninigi {{!}} Japanese deity|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Ninigi|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&q=ninigi&pg=PT91|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
=== Descent to earth === {{Further|Tenson kōrin}}
Ninigi's descent to earth appears in both the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In a earlier version of the [[Nihon Shoki]] Ninigi descends to earth unaccompanied.<ref name=":1" />{{Rp|page=59}}
But in a later version of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki, other gods accompany Ninigi’s journey to earth; who accompanies him and how many depends on the version. But it usually includes the following gods: [[:en:Uzume|Uzume]], [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[Ame-no-Koyane]], [[Futodama]], [[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto|Ishikori dome]], and [[:en:Tamanoya (deity)|Tamanoya]]; many of these deities would later become the ancestors of many clans like {{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}, [[Nakatomi clan]], [[:en:Shinabe clan|Shinabe clan]], and [[Inbe clan]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi&pg=PA59|title=Shinto: A History|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|pages=58-59}}
Ninigi tries to go to earth but he is blocked by [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]. [[Ame-no-Uzume|Uzume]] then persuades Sarutahiko to let Ninigi pass.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA321|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26432-0|page=321}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA4|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|page=4}}</ref>
In most versions Ninigi descents to earth landing on to [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Mt. Takachiho]] located on the island of Kyushu in [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] where Ninigi built his palace.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Eliade|first=Mircea|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H9wjAAAAYAAJ&q=ninigi|title=The Encyclopedia of Religion|date=1987|publisher=Macmillan|isbn=978-0-02-909480-8|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Rambelli|first=Fabio|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NexdDwAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi+descent&pg=PT38|title=The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion|date=2018-07-12|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-350-06287-0|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2019-11-08|title=[Soul of Japan] Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the Kami Who Established A Nation|url=https://japan-forward.com/soul-of-japan-ninigi-no-mikoto-the-kami-who-established-a-nation/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=JAPAN Forward|language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":2" />{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=All the sources don’t give page numbers}}
=== Loss of immortality === One story involves Ninigi looking for a wife; he meets this mountain god named [[Ōyamatsumi|Oyamatsumi]], Oho-Yama presents Ninigi his two daughters [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Konohana]] and [[Iwanaga-hime|Iwa-Naga]]. However, Ninigi rejects Iwa-Naga for her looks and is cursed for rejecting her. Now he and his descendants will live shorter lives.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dqoRQJSHnj4C&q=ninigi&pg=RA6-PA79-IA16|title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan|date=1875|publisher=Asiatic Society of Japan.|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Davis|first=Frederick Hadland|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VqDImrFB_cEC&q=ninigi&pg=PA33|title=Myths and Legends of Japan|date=1992-01-01|publisher=Courier Corporation|isbn=978-0-486-27045-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=Both of these sources don’t provide page numbers.}}
=== Birth of Ninigi’s children === Soon after Ninigi and Konohanasakuya-hime got married, Konohanasakuya-hime got pregnant. Ninigi accused his wife of adultery. In many versions his wife decided to go in to a hut and set the hut on fire to prove that she was a faithful wife. Konohanasakuya-hime and her sons survived, she gave birth to three sons named Hoderi, Hoori, and Hosuseri.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ninigi|isbn = 9781532170706|title = Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date = 15 December 2018|last1 = Gagne|first1 = Tammy}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
One variation says that Konohanasakuya-hime gave birth to Hoderi in the hut and had the other two children later.<ref>[https://www.worldhistory.org/Ninigi/ worldhistory.org]</ref>
=== Death === {{Expand section|date=September 2021}} Later on, Ninigi died and was buried at [[E no Goriyo]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
==Family== {{Further|Family Tree of Japanese deities}}{{Three generations of Hyuga}} Many versions have [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi]] as Ninigi's grandparents, and the son of [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[:en:Yorozuhatahime|Yorozuhatahime]] as his parents.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=114|title = Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Ninigi}}</ref> Ninigi is said to be the nephew to Futotama and Ame-no-Koyane.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=futotama+cousins&pg=PA11|title = Studies in Shinto & Shrines|isbn = 9781136892943|last1 = Ponsonby-Fane|first1 = R. A. B.|date = 3 June 2014}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
Ninigi is in the [[Tenson kōrin|Three generations of Hyuga]] a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== Children ===
Most stories state that Ninigi had three sons [[:en:Hoderi|Hoderi]], Hosuseri, and [[Hoori]]. However, other ancient Japanese texts say that he had four sons and lists "Hikohohodemi no mikoto" as the fourth child of Ninigi. However, it isn’t clear if Hikohohodemi is a fourth child or another name of [[Hoori]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
According to Nihongi, Tamanoya is a offspring of Ninigi.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30|access-date=2020-11-19|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Worship of Ninigi == {{Expand section|date=September 2021}}
=== Shrines === Ninigi has very few temples where he is enshrined.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=Religion+Ninigi&pg=PA324|title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols)|date=2021-03-18|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-90356-4|pages=324|language=en}}</ref>
Shrines like [[:en:Amatsu Shrine|Amatsu Shrine]] and [[Kirishima Jingu|Kirishima-jingu Shrine]] are dedicated to Ninigi.<ref>{{Cite web|date=April 2019|title=Amatsu Shrine {{!}} Discover Itoigawa|url=https://discover-itoigawa.com/see-do/history-culture/amatsu-shrine/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=discover-itoigawa.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Organization|first=Japan National Tourism|title=Kirishima-jingu Shrine {{!}} Kagoshima Attractions {{!}} Travel Japan {{!}} JNTO|url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/597/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Japan Travel|language=en}}</ref> At [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise shrine]], Ninigi is said to be worshipped with [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vtrWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Ninigi+worship&pg=PA132|title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital|last2=Breen|first2=John|date=2017-02-09|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7281-0|pages=132|language=en}}</ref>

{{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|qid=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Hoori|qid=Q1051960}}|father={{ill|Oshihomimi|qid=Q462555}}|mother={{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|qid=Q11110848}}|relatives={{ill|honoakari|qid=Q10940411}}|organization={{ill|Chijin Godai|qid=Q18337308}}; {{ill|Three generations of Hyuga|qid=Q56347075}}|resting_place={{ill|E no Misasagi|qid=Q11411834}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1056017]] Wikidata name: Ninigi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto god}} {{For|the asteroid|10619 Ninigi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Ninigi-no-Mikoto | consorts = [[Konohanasakuya-hime]] (wife) | children = Hoderi, Hosuseri, [[Hoori]], Hikohohodemi, Tamanoya | parents =[[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (father) [[Takuhadachiji-hime]] (mother); {{ill|Oshihomimi|qid=Q462555}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|qid=Q11110848}}| image =Ninigi.png; File:Ninigi.png| gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | consort = {{ill|Konohananosakuya-bime|qid=Q1781862}} | offspring = {{ill|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491}}; {{ill|Honoakari|qid=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|qid=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Hoori|qid=Q1051960}} | member_of = {{ill|Chijin Godai|qid=Q18337308}}; {{ill|Three generations of Hyuga|qid=Q56347075}} | equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Hoori|qid=Q1051960}}; {{ill|Nagarjuna|qid=Q171195}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Ninigi-no-Mikoto'''|瓊瓊杵尊|lead=yes}} is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="Mythopedia2">{{Cite web|title=Ninigi|url=https://mythopedia.com/japanese-mythology/gods/ninigi/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Mythopedia|language=en}}</ref> (-no-Mikoto here is an honorific title applied to the names of Japanese gods; Ninigi is the specific god's name.) Grandson of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Borgen |first1=Robert |last2=Ury |first2=Marian |title=Readable Japanese Mythology: Selections from Nihon shoki and Kojiki |journal=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |date=April 1990 |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=61–97 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/489230 |access-date=12 April 2020 |publisher=American Association of Teachers of Japanese |doi=10.2307/489230 |jstor=489230 |format=PDF}}</ref> Ninigi is regarded according to Japanese mythology as the great-grandfather of Japan’s first emperor, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref name="WillisR">{{cite book |editor1-last=Willis |editor1-first=Roy |title=World Mythology: The Illustrated Guide |date=2006 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0-19530752-8 |pages=114, 116, 120}}</ref><ref name="Mythopedia2" /><ref name="Huffman">{{cite book |last1=Huffman |first1=James L. |title=Japan in World History |date=2010 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=13}}</ref> The [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|three sacred treasures]] brought with Ninigi from Heaven and divine ancestry established the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese Imperial Family]].<ref name="Sykes">{{cite book |last1=Sykes |first1=Egerton |editor1-last=Kendall |editor1-first=Alan |title=Who's Who Non-Classical Mythology |date=1993 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=141}}</ref><ref name="Ashkenazi">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |page=137 |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/136/mode/2up?q=Ninigi |access-date=27 October 2020}}</ref>
The three generations of ''kami'' starting with Ninigi are sometimes referred to as the [[:en:Three generations of Hyūga|Three generations of Hyūga]], they are said to represent a transitional period between the heavenly ''kami'' and the first emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/|access-date=2021-09-13|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Name and etymology == [[File:Ninigi-no-mikoto_statues_at_Kunimigaoka.jpg|thumb|220px|Ninigi-no-mikoto statue at Kunimigaoka Viewpoint]] Ninigi-no-Mikoto (瓊瓊杵尊), means "The Great God Ninigi." Another name of his is '''Ame-nigishi-kuni-nigishi-amatsuhiko-hiko-ho-no-ninigi-no-Mikoto''' (天邇岐志国邇岐志天津日高日子番能邇邇芸命) or "The Great God Ninigi, of the Imperial State, The Child of the Sun of Many Talents." Ninigi is speculated to be translated as "beloved jeweled mallet."<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
== Myths ==
=== Birth === Ninigi was born from [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[Takuhadachiji-hime]]. [[Takamimusubi]] treated him with special affection and nurtured him with great regard.<ref>{{Cite book|chapter-url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_II|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|chapter=Book II }}</ref>
==== Sent to rule ==== Depending on the version Amaterasu sends Ninigi to rule either after his father refuses the offer, after several failures, or to replace [[Ōkuninushi]] after his troubled rule.<ref name="Ashkenazi 2003">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |isbn=9781576074671 |page=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/222 222] |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk |url-access=registration |chapter=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
In many stories, Ninigi receives three gifts. The sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]], the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]], and the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Ninigi {{!}} Japanese deity|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Ninigi|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&q=ninigi&pg=PT91|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
=== Descent to earth === {{Further|Tenson kōrin}}
Ninigi's descent to earth appears in both the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In a earlier version of the [[Nihon Shoki]] Ninigi descends to earth unaccompanied.<ref name=":1" />{{Rp|page=59}}
But in a later version of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki, other gods accompany Ninigi’s journey to earth; who accompanies him and how many depends on the version. But it usually includes the following gods: [[:en:Uzume|Uzume]], [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[Ame-no-Koyane]], [[Futodama]], [[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto|Ishikori dome]], and [[:en:Tamanoya (deity)|Tamanoya]]; many of these deities would later become the ancestors of many clans like {{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}, [[Nakatomi clan]], [[:en:Shinabe clan|Shinabe clan]], and [[Inbe clan]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi&pg=PA59|title=Shinto: A History|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|pages=58-59}}
Ninigi tries to go to earth but he is blocked by [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]. [[Ame-no-Uzume|Uzume]] then persuades Sarutahiko to let Ninigi pass.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA321|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26432-0|page=321}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA4|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|page=4}}</ref>
In most versions Ninigi descents to earth landing on to [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Mt. Takachiho]] located on the island of Kyushu in [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] where Ninigi built his palace.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Eliade|first=Mircea|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H9wjAAAAYAAJ&q=ninigi|title=The Encyclopedia of Religion|date=1987|publisher=Macmillan|isbn=978-0-02-909480-8|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Rambelli|first=Fabio|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NexdDwAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi+descent&pg=PT38|title=The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion|date=2018-07-12|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-350-06287-0|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2019-11-08|title=[Soul of Japan] Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the Kami Who Established A Nation|url=https://japan-forward.com/soul-of-japan-ninigi-no-mikoto-the-kami-who-established-a-nation/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=JAPAN Forward|language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":2" />{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=All the sources don’t give page numbers}}
=== Loss of immortality === One story involves Ninigi looking for a wife; he meets this mountain god named [[Ōyamatsumi|Oyamatsumi]], Oho-Yama presents Ninigi his two daughters [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Konohana]] and [[Iwanaga-hime|Iwa-Naga]]. However, Ninigi rejects Iwa-Naga for her looks and is cursed for rejecting her. Now he and his descendants will live shorter lives.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dqoRQJSHnj4C&q=ninigi&pg=RA6-PA79-IA16|title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan|date=1875|publisher=Asiatic Society of Japan.|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Davis|first=Frederick Hadland|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VqDImrFB_cEC&q=ninigi&pg=PA33|title=Myths and Legends of Japan|date=1992-01-01|publisher=Courier Corporation|isbn=978-0-486-27045-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=Both of these sources don’t provide page numbers.}}
=== Birth of Ninigi’s children === Soon after Ninigi and Konohanasakuya-hime got married, Konohanasakuya-hime got pregnant. Ninigi accused his wife of adultery. In many versions his wife decided to go in to a hut and set the hut on fire to prove that she was a faithful wife. Konohanasakuya-hime and her sons survived, she gave birth to three sons named Hoderi, Hoori, and Hosuseri.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ninigi|isbn = 9781532170706|title = Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date = 15 December 2018|last1 = Gagne|first1 = Tammy}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
One variation says that Konohanasakuya-hime gave birth to Hoderi in the hut and had the other two children later.<ref>[https://www.worldhistory.org/Ninigi/ worldhistory.org]</ref>
=== Death === {{Expand section|date=September 2021}} Later on, Ninigi died and was buried at [[E no Goriyo]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
==Family== {{Further|Family Tree of Japanese deities}}{{Three generations of Hyuga}} Many versions have [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi]] as Ninigi's grandparents, and the son of [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[:en:Yorozuhatahime|Yorozuhatahime]] as his parents.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=114|title = Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Ninigi}}</ref> Ninigi is said to be the nephew to Futotama and Ame-no-Koyane.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=futotama+cousins&pg=PA11|title = Studies in Shinto & Shrines|isbn = 9781136892943|last1 = Ponsonby-Fane|first1 = R. A. B.|date = 3 June 2014}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
Ninigi is in the [[Tenson kōrin|Three generations of Hyuga]] a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== Children ===
Most stories state that Ninigi had three sons [[:en:Hoderi|Hoderi]], Hosuseri, and [[Hoori]]. However, other ancient Japanese texts say that he had four sons and lists "Hikohohodemi no mikoto" as the fourth child of Ninigi. However, it isn’t clear if Hikohohodemi is a fourth child or another name of [[Hoori]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
According to Nihongi, Tamanoya is a offspring of Ninigi.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30|access-date=2020-11-19|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Worship of Ninigi == {{Expand section|date=September 2021}}
=== Shrines === Ninigi has very few temples where he is enshrined.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=Religion+Ninigi&pg=PA324|title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols)|date=2021-03-18|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-90356-4|pages=324|language=en}}</ref>
Shrines like [[:en:Amatsu Shrine|Amatsu Shrine]] and [[Kirishima Jingu|Kirishima-jingu Shrine]] are dedicated to Ninigi.<ref>{{Cite web|date=April 2019|title=Amatsu Shrine {{!}} Discover Itoigawa|url=https://discover-itoigawa.com/see-do/history-culture/amatsu-shrine/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=discover-itoigawa.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Organization|first=Japan National Tourism|title=Kirishima-jingu Shrine {{!}} Kagoshima Attractions {{!}} Travel Japan {{!}} JNTO|url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/597/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Japan Travel|language=en}}</ref> At [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise shrine]], Ninigi is said to be worshipped with [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vtrWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Ninigi+worship&pg=PA132|title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital|last2=Breen|first2=John|date=2017-02-09|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7281-0|pages=132|language=en}}</ref>

{{ill|Oshihomimi|qid=Q462555}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|qid=Q11110848}}| image =Ninigi.png; File:Ninigi.png| gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | consort = {{ill|Konohananosakuya-bime|qid=Q1781862}} | offspring = {{ill|Hoderi|qid=Q2716491}}; {{ill|Nagarjuna|qid=Q171195}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Ninigi-no-Mikoto'''|瓊瓊杵尊|lead=yes}} is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="Mythopedia2">{{Cite web|title=Ninigi|url=https://mythopedia.com/japanese-mythology/gods/ninigi/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Mythopedia|language=en}}</ref> (-no-Mikoto here is an honorific title applied to the names of Japanese gods; Ninigi is the specific god's name.) Grandson of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Borgen |first1=Robert |last2=Ury |first2=Marian |title=Readable Japanese Mythology: Selections from Nihon shoki and Kojiki |journal=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |date=April 1990 |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=61–97 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/489230 |access-date=12 April 2020 |publisher=American Association of Teachers of Japanese |doi=10.2307/489230 |jstor=489230 |format=PDF}}</ref> Ninigi is regarded according to Japanese mythology as the great-grandfather of Japan’s first emperor, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref name="WillisR">{{cite book |editor1-last=Willis |editor1-first=Roy |title=World Mythology: The Illustrated Guide |date=2006 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0-19530752-8 |pages=114, 116, 120}}</ref><ref name="Mythopedia2" /><ref name="Huffman">{{cite book |last1=Huffman |first1=James L. |title=Japan in World History |date=2010 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=13}}</ref> The [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|three sacred treasures]] brought with Ninigi from Heaven and divine ancestry established the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese Imperial Family]].<ref name="Sykes">{{cite book |last1=Sykes |first1=Egerton |editor1-last=Kendall |editor1-first=Alan |title=Who's Who Non-Classical Mythology |date=1993 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=141}}</ref><ref name="Ashkenazi">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |page=137 |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/136/mode/2up?q=Ninigi |access-date=27 October 2020}}</ref>
The three generations of ''kami'' starting with Ninigi are sometimes referred to as the [[:en:Three generations of Hyūga|Three generations of Hyūga]], they are said to represent a transitional period between the heavenly ''kami'' and the first emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/|access-date=2021-09-13|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Name and etymology == [[File:Ninigi-no-mikoto_statues_at_Kunimigaoka.jpg|thumb|220px|Ninigi-no-mikoto statue at Kunimigaoka Viewpoint]] Ninigi-no-Mikoto (瓊瓊杵尊), means "The Great God Ninigi." Another name of his is '''Ame-nigishi-kuni-nigishi-amatsuhiko-hiko-ho-no-ninigi-no-Mikoto''' (天邇岐志国邇岐志天津日高日子番能邇邇芸命) or "The Great God Ninigi, of the Imperial State, The Child of the Sun of Many Talents." Ninigi is speculated to be translated as "beloved jeweled mallet."<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
== Myths ==
=== Birth === Ninigi was born from [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[Takuhadachiji-hime]]. [[Takamimusubi]] treated him with special affection and nurtured him with great regard.<ref>{{Cite book|chapter-url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_II|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|chapter=Book II }}</ref>
==== Sent to rule ==== Depending on the version Amaterasu sends Ninigi to rule either after his father refuses the offer, after several failures, or to replace [[Ōkuninushi]] after his troubled rule.<ref name="Ashkenazi 2003">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |isbn=9781576074671 |page=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/222 222] |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk |url-access=registration |chapter=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
In many stories, Ninigi receives three gifts. The sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]], the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]], and the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Ninigi {{!}} Japanese deity|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Ninigi|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&q=ninigi&pg=PT91|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
=== Descent to earth === {{Further|Tenson kōrin}}
Ninigi's descent to earth appears in both the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In a earlier version of the [[Nihon Shoki]] Ninigi descends to earth unaccompanied.<ref name=":1" />{{Rp|page=59}}
But in a later version of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki, other gods accompany Ninigi’s journey to earth; who accompanies him and how many depends on the version. But it usually includes the following gods: [[:en:Uzume|Uzume]], [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[Ame-no-Koyane]], [[Futodama]], [[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto|Ishikori dome]], and [[:en:Tamanoya (deity)|Tamanoya]]; many of these deities would later become the ancestors of many clans like {{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}, [[Nakatomi clan]], [[:en:Shinabe clan|Shinabe clan]], and [[Inbe clan]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi&pg=PA59|title=Shinto: A History|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|pages=58-59}}
Ninigi tries to go to earth but he is blocked by [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]. [[Ame-no-Uzume|Uzume]] then persuades Sarutahiko to let Ninigi pass.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA321|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26432-0|page=321}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA4|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|page=4}}</ref>
In most versions Ninigi descents to earth landing on to [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Mt. Takachiho]] located on the island of Kyushu in [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] where Ninigi built his palace.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Eliade|first=Mircea|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H9wjAAAAYAAJ&q=ninigi|title=The Encyclopedia of Religion|date=1987|publisher=Macmillan|isbn=978-0-02-909480-8|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Rambelli|first=Fabio|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NexdDwAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi+descent&pg=PT38|title=The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion|date=2018-07-12|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-350-06287-0|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2019-11-08|title=[Soul of Japan] Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the Kami Who Established A Nation|url=https://japan-forward.com/soul-of-japan-ninigi-no-mikoto-the-kami-who-established-a-nation/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=JAPAN Forward|language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":2" />{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=All the sources don’t give page numbers}}
=== Loss of immortality === One story involves Ninigi looking for a wife; he meets this mountain god named [[Ōyamatsumi|Oyamatsumi]], Oho-Yama presents Ninigi his two daughters [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Konohana]] and [[Iwanaga-hime|Iwa-Naga]]. However, Ninigi rejects Iwa-Naga for her looks and is cursed for rejecting her. Now he and his descendants will live shorter lives.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dqoRQJSHnj4C&q=ninigi&pg=RA6-PA79-IA16|title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan|date=1875|publisher=Asiatic Society of Japan.|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Davis|first=Frederick Hadland|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VqDImrFB_cEC&q=ninigi&pg=PA33|title=Myths and Legends of Japan|date=1992-01-01|publisher=Courier Corporation|isbn=978-0-486-27045-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=Both of these sources don’t provide page numbers.}}
=== Birth of Ninigi’s children === Soon after Ninigi and Konohanasakuya-hime got married, Konohanasakuya-hime got pregnant. Ninigi accused his wife of adultery. In many versions his wife decided to go in to a hut and set the hut on fire to prove that she was a faithful wife. Konohanasakuya-hime and her sons survived, she gave birth to three sons named Hoderi, Hoori, and Hosuseri.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ninigi|isbn = 9781532170706|title = Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date = 15 December 2018|last1 = Gagne|first1 = Tammy}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
One variation says that Konohanasakuya-hime gave birth to Hoderi in the hut and had the other two children later.<ref>[https://www.worldhistory.org/Ninigi/ worldhistory.org]</ref>
=== Death === {{Expand section|date=September 2021}} Later on, Ninigi died and was buried at [[E no Goriyo]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
==Family== {{Further|Family Tree of Japanese deities}}{{Three generations of Hyuga}} Many versions have [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi]] as Ninigi's grandparents, and the son of [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[:en:Yorozuhatahime|Yorozuhatahime]] as his parents.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=114|title = Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Ninigi}}</ref> Ninigi is said to be the nephew to Futotama and Ame-no-Koyane.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=futotama+cousins&pg=PA11|title = Studies in Shinto & Shrines|isbn = 9781136892943|last1 = Ponsonby-Fane|first1 = R. A. B.|date = 3 June 2014}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
Ninigi is in the [[Tenson kōrin|Three generations of Hyuga]] a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== Children ===
Most stories state that Ninigi had three sons [[:en:Hoderi|Hoderi]], Hosuseri, and [[Hoori]]. However, other ancient Japanese texts say that he had four sons and lists "Hikohohodemi no mikoto" as the fourth child of Ninigi. However, it isn’t clear if Hikohohodemi is a fourth child or another name of [[Hoori]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
According to Nihongi, Tamanoya is a offspring of Ninigi.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30|access-date=2020-11-19|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Worship of Ninigi == {{Expand section|date=September 2021}}
=== Shrines === Ninigi has very few temples where he is enshrined.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=Religion+Ninigi&pg=PA324|title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols)|date=2021-03-18|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-90356-4|pages=324|language=en}}</ref>
Shrines like [[:en:Amatsu Shrine|Amatsu Shrine]] and [[Kirishima Jingu|Kirishima-jingu Shrine]] are dedicated to Ninigi.<ref>{{Cite web|date=April 2019|title=Amatsu Shrine {{!}} Discover Itoigawa|url=https://discover-itoigawa.com/see-do/history-culture/amatsu-shrine/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=discover-itoigawa.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Organization|first=Japan National Tourism|title=Kirishima-jingu Shrine {{!}} Kagoshima Attractions {{!}} Travel Japan {{!}} JNTO|url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/597/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Japan Travel|language=en}}</ref> At [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise shrine]], Ninigi is said to be worshipped with [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vtrWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Ninigi+worship&pg=PA132|title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital|last2=Breen|first2=John|date=2017-02-09|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7281-0|pages=132|language=en}}</ref>

{{ill|Nagarjuna|qid=Q171195}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Ninigi-no-Mikoto'''|瓊瓊杵尊|lead=yes}} is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="Mythopedia2">{{Cite web|title=Ninigi|url=https://mythopedia.com/japanese-mythology/gods/ninigi/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Mythopedia|language=en}}</ref> (-no-Mikoto here is an honorific title applied to the names of Japanese gods; Ninigi is the specific god's name.) Grandson of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Borgen |first1=Robert |last2=Ury |first2=Marian |title=Readable Japanese Mythology: Selections from Nihon shoki and Kojiki |journal=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |date=April 1990 |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=61–97 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/489230 |access-date=12 April 2020 |publisher=American Association of Teachers of Japanese |doi=10.2307/489230 |jstor=489230 |format=PDF}}</ref> Ninigi is regarded according to Japanese mythology as the great-grandfather of Japan’s first emperor, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref name="WillisR">{{cite book |editor1-last=Willis |editor1-first=Roy |title=World Mythology: The Illustrated Guide |date=2006 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0-19530752-8 |pages=114, 116, 120}}</ref><ref name="Mythopedia2" /><ref name="Huffman">{{cite book |last1=Huffman |first1=James L. |title=Japan in World History |date=2010 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=13}}</ref> The [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|three sacred treasures]] brought with Ninigi from Heaven and divine ancestry established the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese Imperial Family]].<ref name="Sykes">{{cite book |last1=Sykes |first1=Egerton |editor1-last=Kendall |editor1-first=Alan |title=Who's Who Non-Classical Mythology |date=1993 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=141}}</ref><ref name="Ashkenazi">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |page=137 |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/136/mode/2up?q=Ninigi |access-date=27 October 2020}}</ref>
The three generations of ''kami'' starting with Ninigi are sometimes referred to as the [[:en:Three generations of Hyūga|Three generations of Hyūga]], they are said to represent a transitional period between the heavenly ''kami'' and the first emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/|access-date=2021-09-13|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Name and etymology == [[File:Ninigi-no-mikoto_statues_at_Kunimigaoka.jpg|thumb|220px|Ninigi-no-mikoto statue at Kunimigaoka Viewpoint]] Ninigi-no-Mikoto (瓊瓊杵尊), means "The Great God Ninigi." Another name of his is '''Ame-nigishi-kuni-nigishi-amatsuhiko-hiko-ho-no-ninigi-no-Mikoto''' (天邇岐志国邇岐志天津日高日子番能邇邇芸命) or "The Great God Ninigi, of the Imperial State, The Child of the Sun of Many Talents." Ninigi is speculated to be translated as "beloved jeweled mallet."<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
== Myths ==
=== Birth === Ninigi was born from [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[Takuhadachiji-hime]]. [[Takamimusubi]] treated him with special affection and nurtured him with great regard.<ref>{{Cite book|chapter-url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_II|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|chapter=Book II }}</ref>
==== Sent to rule ==== Depending on the version Amaterasu sends Ninigi to rule either after his father refuses the offer, after several failures, or to replace [[Ōkuninushi]] after his troubled rule.<ref name="Ashkenazi 2003">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |isbn=9781576074671 |page=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/222 222] |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk |url-access=registration |chapter=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
In many stories, Ninigi receives three gifts. The sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]], the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]], and the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Ninigi {{!}} Japanese deity|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Ninigi|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&q=ninigi&pg=PT91|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
=== Descent to earth === {{Further|Tenson kōrin}}
Ninigi's descent to earth appears in both the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In a earlier version of the [[Nihon Shoki]] Ninigi descends to earth unaccompanied.<ref name=":1" />{{Rp|page=59}}
But in a later version of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki, other gods accompany Ninigi’s journey to earth; who accompanies him and how many depends on the version. But it usually includes the following gods: [[:en:Uzume|Uzume]], [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[Ame-no-Koyane]], [[Futodama]], [[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto|Ishikori dome]], and [[:en:Tamanoya (deity)|Tamanoya]]; many of these deities would later become the ancestors of many clans like {{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}, [[Nakatomi clan]], [[:en:Shinabe clan|Shinabe clan]], and [[Inbe clan]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi&pg=PA59|title=Shinto: A History|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|pages=58-59}}
Ninigi tries to go to earth but he is blocked by [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]. [[Ame-no-Uzume|Uzume]] then persuades Sarutahiko to let Ninigi pass.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA321|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26432-0|page=321}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA4|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|page=4}}</ref>
In most versions Ninigi descents to earth landing on to [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Mt. Takachiho]] located on the island of Kyushu in [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] where Ninigi built his palace.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Eliade|first=Mircea|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H9wjAAAAYAAJ&q=ninigi|title=The Encyclopedia of Religion|date=1987|publisher=Macmillan|isbn=978-0-02-909480-8|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Rambelli|first=Fabio|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NexdDwAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi+descent&pg=PT38|title=The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion|date=2018-07-12|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-350-06287-0|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2019-11-08|title=[Soul of Japan] Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the Kami Who Established A Nation|url=https://japan-forward.com/soul-of-japan-ninigi-no-mikoto-the-kami-who-established-a-nation/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=JAPAN Forward|language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":2" />{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=All the sources don’t give page numbers}}
=== Loss of immortality === One story involves Ninigi looking for a wife; he meets this mountain god named [[Ōyamatsumi|Oyamatsumi]], Oho-Yama presents Ninigi his two daughters [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Konohana]] and [[Iwanaga-hime|Iwa-Naga]]. However, Ninigi rejects Iwa-Naga for her looks and is cursed for rejecting her. Now he and his descendants will live shorter lives.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dqoRQJSHnj4C&q=ninigi&pg=RA6-PA79-IA16|title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan|date=1875|publisher=Asiatic Society of Japan.|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Davis|first=Frederick Hadland|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VqDImrFB_cEC&q=ninigi&pg=PA33|title=Myths and Legends of Japan|date=1992-01-01|publisher=Courier Corporation|isbn=978-0-486-27045-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=Both of these sources don’t provide page numbers.}}
=== Birth of Ninigi’s children === Soon after Ninigi and Konohanasakuya-hime got married, Konohanasakuya-hime got pregnant. Ninigi accused his wife of adultery. In many versions his wife decided to go in to a hut and set the hut on fire to prove that she was a faithful wife. Konohanasakuya-hime and her sons survived, she gave birth to three sons named Hoderi, Hoori, and Hosuseri.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ninigi|isbn = 9781532170706|title = Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date = 15 December 2018|last1 = Gagne|first1 = Tammy}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
One variation says that Konohanasakuya-hime gave birth to Hoderi in the hut and had the other two children later.<ref>[https://www.worldhistory.org/Ninigi/ worldhistory.org]</ref>
=== Death === {{Expand section|date=September 2021}} Later on, Ninigi died and was buried at [[E no Goriyo]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
==Family== {{Further|Family Tree of Japanese deities}}{{Three generations of Hyuga}} Many versions have [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi]] as Ninigi's grandparents, and the son of [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[:en:Yorozuhatahime|Yorozuhatahime]] as his parents.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=114|title = Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Ninigi}}</ref> Ninigi is said to be the nephew to Futotama and Ame-no-Koyane.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=futotama+cousins&pg=PA11|title = Studies in Shinto & Shrines|isbn = 9781136892943|last1 = Ponsonby-Fane|first1 = R. A. B.|date = 3 June 2014}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
Ninigi is in the [[Tenson kōrin|Three generations of Hyuga]] a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== Children ===
Most stories state that Ninigi had three sons [[:en:Hoderi|Hoderi]], Hosuseri, and [[Hoori]]. However, other ancient Japanese texts say that he had four sons and lists "Hikohohodemi no mikoto" as the fourth child of Ninigi. However, it isn’t clear if Hikohohodemi is a fourth child or another name of [[Hoori]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
According to Nihongi, Tamanoya is a offspring of Ninigi.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30|access-date=2020-11-19|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Worship of Ninigi == {{Expand section|date=September 2021}}
=== Shrines === Ninigi has very few temples where he is enshrined.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=Religion+Ninigi&pg=PA324|title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols)|date=2021-03-18|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-90356-4|pages=324|language=en}}</ref>
Shrines like [[:en:Amatsu Shrine|Amatsu Shrine]] and [[Kirishima Jingu|Kirishima-jingu Shrine]] are dedicated to Ninigi.<ref>{{Cite web|date=April 2019|title=Amatsu Shrine {{!}} Discover Itoigawa|url=https://discover-itoigawa.com/see-do/history-culture/amatsu-shrine/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=discover-itoigawa.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Organization|first=Japan National Tourism|title=Kirishima-jingu Shrine {{!}} Kagoshima Attractions {{!}} Travel Japan {{!}} JNTO|url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/597/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Japan Travel|language=en}}</ref> At [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise shrine]], Ninigi is said to be worshipped with [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vtrWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Ninigi+worship&pg=PA132|title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital|last2=Breen|first2=John|date=2017-02-09|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7281-0|pages=132|language=en}}</ref>

{{ill|Oshihomimi|WD=Q462555}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848}}| image =Ninigi.png; File:Ninigi.png| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | consort = {{ill|Konohananosakuya-bime|WD=Q1781862}} | offspring = {{ill|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491}}; {{ill|Nagarjuna|WD=Q171195}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Ninigi-no-Mikoto'''|瓊瓊杵尊|lead=yes}} is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="Mythopedia2">{{Cite web|title=Ninigi|url=https://mythopedia.com/japanese-mythology/gods/ninigi/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Mythopedia|language=en}}</ref> (-no-Mikoto here is an honorific title applied to the names of Japanese gods; Ninigi is the specific god's name.) Grandson of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Borgen |first1=Robert |last2=Ury |first2=Marian |title=Readable Japanese Mythology: Selections from Nihon shoki and Kojiki |journal=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |date=April 1990 |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=61–97 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/489230 |access-date=12 April 2020 |publisher=American Association of Teachers of Japanese |doi=10.2307/489230 |jstor=489230 |format=PDF}}</ref> Ninigi is regarded according to Japanese mythology as the great-grandfather of Japan’s first emperor, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].<ref name="WillisR">{{cite book |editor1-last=Willis |editor1-first=Roy |title=World Mythology: The Illustrated Guide |date=2006 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |isbn=978-0-19530752-8 |pages=114, 116, 120}}</ref><ref name="Mythopedia2" /><ref name="Huffman">{{cite book |last1=Huffman |first1=James L. |title=Japan in World History |date=2010 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=13}}</ref> The [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|three sacred treasures]] brought with Ninigi from Heaven and divine ancestry established the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Japanese Imperial Family]].<ref name="Sykes">{{cite book |last1=Sykes |first1=Egerton |editor1-last=Kendall |editor1-first=Alan |title=Who's Who Non-Classical Mythology |date=1993 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=New York |page=141}}</ref><ref name="Ashkenazi">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |page=137 |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/136/mode/2up?q=Ninigi |access-date=27 October 2020}}</ref>
The three generations of ''kami'' starting with Ninigi are sometimes referred to as the [[:en:Three generations of Hyūga|Three generations of Hyūga]], they are said to represent a transitional period between the heavenly ''kami'' and the first emperor.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/|access-date=2021-09-13|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Name and etymology == [[File:Ninigi-no-mikoto_statues_at_Kunimigaoka.jpg|thumb|220px|Ninigi-no-mikoto statue at Kunimigaoka Viewpoint]] Ninigi-no-Mikoto (瓊瓊杵尊), means "The Great God Ninigi." Another name of his is '''Ame-nigishi-kuni-nigishi-amatsuhiko-hiko-ho-no-ninigi-no-Mikoto''' (天邇岐志国邇岐志天津日高日子番能邇邇芸命) or "The Great God Ninigi, of the Imperial State, The Child of the Sun of Many Talents." Ninigi is speculated to be translated as "beloved jeweled mallet."<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
== Myths ==
=== Birth === Ninigi was born from [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[Takuhadachiji-hime]]. [[Takamimusubi]] treated him with special affection and nurtured him with great regard.<ref>{{Cite book|chapter-url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_II|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|chapter=Book II }}</ref>
==== Sent to rule ==== Depending on the version Amaterasu sends Ninigi to rule either after his father refuses the offer, after several failures, or to replace [[Ōkuninushi]] after his troubled rule.<ref name="Ashkenazi 2003">{{cite book |last1=Ashkenazi |first1=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |location=Santa Barbara, California |isbn=9781576074671 |page=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/222 222] |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk |url-access=registration |chapter=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
In many stories, Ninigi receives three gifts. The sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]], the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]], and the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Ninigi {{!}} Japanese deity|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Ninigi|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&q=ninigi&pg=PT91|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
=== Descent to earth === {{Further|Tenson kōrin}}
Ninigi's descent to earth appears in both the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In a earlier version of the [[Nihon Shoki]] Ninigi descends to earth unaccompanied.<ref name=":1" />{{Rp|page=59}}
But in a later version of the Nihon Shoki and the Kojiki, other gods accompany Ninigi’s journey to earth; who accompanies him and how many depends on the version. But it usually includes the following gods: [[:en:Uzume|Uzume]], [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]], [[Ame-no-Koyane]], [[Futodama]], [[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto|Ishikori dome]], and [[:en:Tamanoya (deity)|Tamanoya]]; many of these deities would later become the ancestors of many clans like {{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}, [[Nakatomi clan]], [[:en:Shinabe clan|Shinabe clan]], and [[Inbe clan]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi&pg=PA59|title=Shinto: A History|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|pages=58-59}}
Ninigi tries to go to earth but he is blocked by [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]. [[Ame-no-Uzume|Uzume]] then persuades Sarutahiko to let Ninigi pass.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA321|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26432-0|page=321}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA4|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|page=4}}</ref>
In most versions Ninigi descents to earth landing on to [[:en:Takachiho, Miyazaki|Mt. Takachiho]] located on the island of Kyushu in [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] where Ninigi built his palace.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Eliade|first=Mircea|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H9wjAAAAYAAJ&q=ninigi|title=The Encyclopedia of Religion|date=1987|publisher=Macmillan|isbn=978-0-02-909480-8|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Rambelli|first=Fabio|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NexdDwAAQBAJ&q=Ninigi+descent&pg=PT38|title=The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion|date=2018-07-12|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-350-06287-0|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|date=2019-11-08|title=[Soul of Japan] Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the Kami Who Established A Nation|url=https://japan-forward.com/soul-of-japan-ninigi-no-mikoto-the-kami-who-established-a-nation/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=JAPAN Forward|language=en-US}}</ref><ref name=":2" />{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=All the sources don’t give page numbers}}
=== Loss of immortality === One story involves Ninigi looking for a wife; he meets this mountain god named [[Ōyamatsumi|Oyamatsumi]], Oho-Yama presents Ninigi his two daughters [[Konohanasakuya-hime|Konohana]] and [[Iwanaga-hime|Iwa-Naga]]. However, Ninigi rejects Iwa-Naga for her looks and is cursed for rejecting her. Now he and his descendants will live shorter lives.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dqoRQJSHnj4C&q=ninigi&pg=RA6-PA79-IA16|title=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan|date=1875|publisher=Asiatic Society of Japan.|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Davis|first=Frederick Hadland|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VqDImrFB_cEC&q=ninigi&pg=PA33|title=Myths and Legends of Japan|date=1992-01-01|publisher=Courier Corporation|isbn=978-0-486-27045-6|language=en}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021|reason=Both of these sources don’t provide page numbers.}}
=== Birth of Ninigi’s children === Soon after Ninigi and Konohanasakuya-hime got married, Konohanasakuya-hime got pregnant. Ninigi accused his wife of adultery. In many versions his wife decided to go in to a hut and set the hut on fire to prove that she was a faithful wife. Konohanasakuya-hime and her sons survived, she gave birth to three sons named Hoderi, Hoori, and Hosuseri.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W2t9DwAAQBAJ&q=ninigi|isbn = 9781532170706|title = Japanese Gods, Heroes, and Mythology|date = 15 December 2018|last1 = Gagne|first1 = Tammy}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
One variation says that Konohanasakuya-hime gave birth to Hoderi in the hut and had the other two children later.<ref>[https://www.worldhistory.org/Ninigi/ worldhistory.org]</ref>
=== Death === {{Expand section|date=September 2021}} Later on, Ninigi died and was buried at [[E no Goriyo]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
==Family== {{Further|Family Tree of Japanese deities}}{{Three generations of Hyuga}} Many versions have [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi]] as Ninigi's grandparents, and the son of [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] and [[:en:Yorozuhatahime|Yorozuhatahime]] as his parents.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=114|title = Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Ninigi}}</ref> Ninigi is said to be the nephew to Futotama and Ame-no-Koyane.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&q=futotama+cousins&pg=PA11|title = Studies in Shinto & Shrines|isbn = 9781136892943|last1 = Ponsonby-Fane|first1 = R. A. B.|date = 3 June 2014}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=August 2021}}
Ninigi is in the [[Tenson kōrin|Three generations of Hyuga]] a time period between [[Tenson kōrin]] and [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-08-04 |title=みやざきの神話と伝承101：概説 |url=https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |access-date=2022-06-12 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210804121336/https://www.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/contents/org/chiiki/seikatu/miyazaki101/shinwa_densho/outline.html |archive-date=4 August 2021 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== Children ===
Most stories state that Ninigi had three sons [[:en:Hoderi|Hoderi]], Hosuseri, and [[Hoori]]. However, other ancient Japanese texts say that he had four sons and lists "Hikohohodemi no mikoto" as the fourth child of Ninigi. However, it isn’t clear if Hikohohodemi is a fourth child or another name of [[Hoori]].<ref name="Mythopedia2" />
According to Nihongi, Tamanoya is a offspring of Ninigi.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenoakarutama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=30|access-date=2020-11-19|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Worship of Ninigi == {{Expand section|date=September 2021}}
=== Shrines === Ninigi has very few temples where he is enshrined.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=Religion+Ninigi&pg=PA324|title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols)|date=2021-03-18|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-90356-4|pages=324|language=en}}</ref>
Shrines like [[:en:Amatsu Shrine|Amatsu Shrine]] and [[Kirishima Jingu|Kirishima-jingu Shrine]] are dedicated to Ninigi.<ref>{{Cite web|date=April 2019|title=Amatsu Shrine {{!}} Discover Itoigawa|url=https://discover-itoigawa.com/see-do/history-culture/amatsu-shrine/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=discover-itoigawa.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Organization|first=Japan National Tourism|title=Kirishima-jingu Shrine {{!}} Kagoshima Attractions {{!}} Travel Japan {{!}} JNTO|url=https://www.japan.travel/en/spot/597/|access-date=2020-09-30|website=Japan Travel|language=en}}</ref> At [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise shrine]], Ninigi is said to be worshipped with [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]].<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Teeuwen|first1=Mark|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vtrWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Ninigi+worship&pg=PA132|title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital|last2=Breen|first2=John|date=2017-02-09|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4742-7281-0|pages=132|language=en}}</ref>
